[[2010 Wigan Warriors season|2010]] | list = yes | list_link = List of Wigan Warriors seasons }} [[Wigan Warriors]] played in the [[Super League XIV]] and [[Challenge Cup]] in the 2009 season. ==Background== {{See also|History of Wigan Warriors}} Following Brian Noble's move from the [[Bradford Bulls]] to the Wigan Warriors in 2006, his third full season at the club took place in 2009.{{Cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/wigan/4925888.stm|title=Noble leaves Bulls to join Wigan|publisher=[[British Broadcasting Corporation|BBC]]|date=20 April 2006|access-date=14 September 2009}} The season was also the first in which the [[Super League]] operated under a [[2009–11 Super League licences|franchise system]], after the Wigan Warriors were granted a 'B' license.{{Cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/7519770.stm|title=Three clubs given A grade licence|publisher=[[British Broadcasting Corporation|BBC]]|date=23 July 2008|access-date=14 September 2009}} The [[salary cap]], which the club broke in [[Super League XII|2007]],{{Cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/wigan-25-bradford-18-barrett-helps-give-wigan-welcome-lift-456339.html |title=Wigan 25 Bradford 18: Barrett helps give Wigan welcome lift |work=[[The Independent]]|date=7 July 2007 |access-date=14 September 2009 |last=Hadfield |first=Dave |archive-url=https://archive.today/20090917211135/http://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/wigan-25-bradford-18-barrett-helps-give-wigan-welcome-lift-456339.html |archive-date=17 September 2009 |url-status=dead |location=London}} was still in effect for this season, and the limit remained at [[Pound sterling|GBP£]]1.7 million;{{Cite web|url=http://www.crainsmanchesterbusiness.co.uk/apps/pbcs.dll/article?AID=/20080728/FREE/210381473/1166/-/-/licence-is-wilkinsons-reward-for-years-of-support |title=Licence is Wilkinson's reward for years of support |publisher=Crain's Manchester Business |date=28 June 2008 |access-date=14 September 2009 |last=Chapelard |first=James |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150923210957/http://www.crainsmanchesterbusiness.co.uk/apps/pbcs.dll/article?AID=/20080728/FREE/210381473/1166/-/-/licence-is-wilkinsons-reward-for-years-of-support |archive-date=23 September 2015 }} the cap was maintained 'live', in continuation of an operational change by the [[Rugby Football League]] in 2008.{{Cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/page.php?id=346 |title=Competition Structure |publisher=[[Rugby Football League]] |access-date=14 September 2009 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090723201335/http://www.superleague.co.uk/page.php?id=346 |archive-date=23 July 2009 }} As an ever-present club in the Super League format, this season was the Wigan Warriors' fourteenth year in the competition.{{Cite web|url=http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/teams/Wigan_Warriors/seasons.html |title=Wigan Warriors – Seasons |publisher=Rugby League Project |access-date=14 September 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110727224905/http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/teams/Wigan_Warriors/seasons.html |archive-date=27 July 2011 |url-status=live}} This year also marked the tenth anniversary of [[rugby league]] at the [[JJB Stadium]], after the club moved there upon the stadium's completion in 1999.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=37|title=DW Stadium – Facts & Figures|access-date=14 September 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110314191638/http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=37|archive-date=14 March 2011|url-status=dead}} In contrast to the club's historical success,{{Cite web|url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/club.php?id=33507 |title=Club profile |publisher=[[Rugby Football League]] |access-date=14 September 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090818022601/http://www.superleague.co.uk/club.php?id=33507 |archive-date=18 August 2009 |url-status=dead}} Wigan Warriors had not won a major trophy since 2002, when they defeated [[St Helens R.F.C.]] 12–21 in the [[2002 Challenge Cup|Challenge Cup]] final.{{Cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/3026799/Wigan-recall-glory-days.html|title=Wigan recall glory days|work=[[The Daily Telegraph]]|date=28 April 2002|access-date=14 September 2009|last=Whalley|first=John | location=London}} They had only won the Super League title once, in 1998. In the [[Super League XIII|previous season]] the Wigan Warriors finished fourth in the league table meaning they qualified for the play-offs,{{Cite web |url=http://www.superleague.co.uk/table08.php |title=Super League XIII table |publisher=[[Rugby Football League]] |access-date=14 September 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090723225035/http://www.superleague.co.uk/table08.php |archive-date=23 July 2009 |url-status=dead}} and after defeating Bradford Bulls and [[Catalans Dragons]],{{Cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/7605926.stm|title=Wigan 30–14|publisher=[[British Broadcasting Corporation|BBC]]|date=12 September 2008|access-date=14 September 2009}}{{Cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/blog/2008/sep/22/wiganwarriors.leedsrhinos|title=Warrior mentality edges Wigan towards grand final|work=[[The Guardian]]|date=22 September 2008|access-date=14 September 2009|last=Wilson|first=Andy | location=London}} the club fell short of the [[2008 Super League Grand Final]] after losing to [[Leeds Rhinos]] at [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley]] for the second year running.{{Cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7629521.stm|title=Leeds 18–14 Wigan|publisher=[[British Broadcasting Corporation|BBC]]|date=26 September 2008|access-date=14 September 2009}} ==Pre-season== Following their knock-out at the hands of Leeds, the task of acquiring new players began. 2008 was influential [[Rugby league positions#Stand-off|stand-off]] [[Trent Barrett]]'s last season for the Warriors, after he signed for the [[Cronulla Sharks]] for the [[2009 NRL season|2009 National Rugby League (NRL) season]];{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Legends-hail-departing-Barrett-as.4551041.jp |title=Legends hail departing Barrett as one of the best |publisher=Wigan Today |date=2 October 2008 |access-date=16 September 2009 |last=Wilkinson |first=Phil |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081206002349/http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Legends-hail-departing-Barrett-as.4551041.jp |archive-date=6 December 2008 |url-status=live}} Barrett himself insisted that [[Tim Smith (rugby league)|Tim Smith]] could replace him, after Smith was released by [[Parramatta Eels]] following an episode of [[bipolar disorder]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Barrett-backs-Smith-to-fill.4545126.jp|title=Barrett backs Smith to fill his shoes|publisher=Wigan Today|date=1 October 2008|access-date=16 September 2009|last=Aspinall|first=Peter|archive-date=5 December 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081205043812/http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Barrett-backs-Smith-to-fill.4545126.jp|url-status=dead}}{{Cite web |url=http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,24004127-23214,00.html |title=Smith set for Wigan debut |work=[[Fox Sports (Australia)|Fox Sports]] |date=11 July 2008 |access-date=16 September 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080805020424/http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,24004127-23214,00.html |archive-date=5 August 2008 |url-status=live}} It was also to be the last season for [[Liam Colbon]] and [[Mark Calderwood]], who both transferred away from the club.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Noble-still-looking.4829815.jp |title=Noble still looking |publisher=Wigan Today |date=30 December 2008 |access-date=16 September 2009 |last=Dewhurst |first=Tony |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081231225612/http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Noble-still-looking.4829815.jp |archive-date=31 December 2008 |url-status=live}} An attempt to sign the [[Canberra Raiders]]' [[Todd Carney]] ended after [[Huddersfield Giants]] signed him instead.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Lenagan-off-on-talent-search.4561455.jp|title=Lenagan off on talent search|publisher=Wigan Today|date=6 October 2008|access-date=16 September 2009|last=Aspinall|first=Peter}} The club prevented a possible cross-code transfer with [[George Carmont]] to [[rugby union]] by extending his contract.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Carmont-to-stay-a-Warrior.4588526.jp |title=Carmont to stay a Warrior |publisher=Wigan Today |date=14 October 2008 |access-date=16 September 2009 |last=Aspinall |first=Peter |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120312073544/http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Carmont-to-stay-a-Warrior.4588526.jp |archive-date=12 March 2012 |url-status=live}} Wigan eventually signed their first new player with former [[Sydney Roosters]] player [[Amos Roberts]], who was released from his contract a year prematurely,{{Cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_4368895,00.html|title=Roberts heads to Wigan|publisher=[[Sky Sports]]|date=22 October 2008|access-date=16 September 2009}} but lost another player to the [[Warrington Wolves]] in [[Mickey Higham]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Higham-gutted-over-exit.4634624.jp |title=Higham gutted over exit |publisher=Wigan Today |date=28 October 2008 |access-date=16 September 2009 |last=Dewhurst |first=Tony |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120918114713/http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Higham-gutted-over-exit.4634624.jp |archive-date=18 September 2012 |url-status=live}} [[Joel Tomkins]], a [[Wigan Warriors Youth Development]] product, also extended his contract until the end of the [[Super League XVI|2011 season]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=3515&type=news|title=Joel Tomkins signs extended deal|publisher=[[Wigan Warriors]]|date=14 November 2008|access-date=16 September 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110718044014/http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=3515&type=news|archive-date=18 July 2011|url-status=dead}} Rumours about a move for [[Ryan McGoldrick]] ended after he publicly ruled out any possible transfer.{{Cite web|url=http://www.pontefractandcastlefordexpress.co.uk/sport/McGoldrick-quashes-Wigan-rumours.4735076.jp |title=McGoldrick quashes Wigan rumours |publisher=Pontefract & Castleford Express |date=26 November 2008 |access-date=16 September 2009 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120907134738/http://www.pontefractandcastlefordexpress.co.uk/sport/McGoldrick-quashes-Wigan-rumours.4735076.jp |archive-date=7 September 2012 |url-status=live}} The question over Barrett's successor in the stand-off position was again raised when Tim Smith, who was on holiday in his native Australia at the time, approached the Cronulla Sharks seeking a return to the [[NRL]], although the club did not pursue any deal with Smith and he therefore stayed at Wigan for the 2009 season.{{Cite web|url=http://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/homesick-smith-keen-to-head-home/story-e6frfgdx-1111118242327 |title=Homesick Smith keen to head home |publisher=Herald Sun |date=7 December 2008 |access-date=18 September 2009 |last=Riccio |first=David |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722204558/http://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/homesick-smith-keen-to-head-home/story-e6frfgdx-1111118242327 |archive-date=22 July 2011 |url-status=live}} [[Greg Bird (rugby league)|Greg Bird]], who had been suspended by Cronulla Sharks for an alleged assault on his girlfriend was also cited by [[The Independent]] as a target for the Warriors, but any plans of a transfer were initially denied by the club's chairman, [[Ian Lenagan]].{{Cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/wigan-deny-bird-transfer-rumours-1215992.html |title=Wigan deny Bird transfer rumours |work=[[The Independent]]|date=30 December 2008 |access-date=18 September 2009 |last=Hadfield |first=Dave |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081231184950/http://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/wigan-deny-bird-transfer-rumours-1215992.html |archive-date=31 December 2008 |url-status=dead |location=London}} On 30 January 2009, Lenagan confirmed it was unlikely there would be any more signings for the new season, having acquired two new players (Amos Roberts and [[Mark Riddell]]), after attempts to sign [[Richard Moore (rugby league)|Richard Moore]] from [[Wakefield Trinity|Wakefield Trinity Wildcats]] failed.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/No-signings-before-season-starts.4930433.jp|title=No signings before season starts|publisher=Wigan Today|date=30 January 2009|access-date=18 September 2009|last=Wilkinson|first=Phil}} The [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]] was held in Australia during the off-season, and several Wigan players from the 2008 season were involved in matches concerning their own international teams. [[Pat Richards]], [[Eamon O'Carroll]] and [[Michael McIlorum]] were called by the [[Ireland national rugby league team|Irish team]], with [[Paul Prescott]] missing out due to a back injury.{{Cite web|url=http://www.rte.ie/sport/rugby/2008/1007/ireland_rugbyleague.html|title=Kelly opts for youth & experience |publisher=[[RTÉ Sport]]|date=7 October 2008|access-date=18 September 2009}} Carmont and [[Harrison Hansen]] were selected for the [[Samoa national rugby league team|Samoan national team]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.rlwc08.com/news/news/archive/2008/08/05/samoan-preliminary-squad.aspx|title=Samoan preliminary squad|publisher=[[Rugby League International Federation]]|date=5 August 2008|access-date=18 September 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081206023344/http://www.rlwc08.com/news/news/archive/2008/08/05/samoan-preliminary-squad.aspx|archive-date=6 December 2008|url-status=dead}} [[Stuart Fielden]] was omitted from the [[England national rugby league team|England squad]] but Calderwood, Higham and [[Gareth Hock]] all received places.{{Cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2008/oct/08/rugbyleagueworldcup2008.englandrugbyleagueteam|title=Rugby league: England opt for Calderwood and Higham|first=Andy|last=Wilson|date=7 October 2008|work=The Guardian}} [[Thomas Leuluai]] became the only [[Super League]] player from the 2008 season to win the World Cup when the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand team]] he played [[Rugby league positions#Hooker|hooker]] for defeated the [[Australia national rugby league team|Australian team]] 20–34 in the final.{{Cite web|url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/rugby/2008/11/24/wigan-will-gain-from-world-cup-win-says-thomas-leuluai-115875-20920155/ |title=Wigan will gain from World Cup win, says Thomas Leuluai|work=[[Daily Mirror|The Mirror]]|date=24 November 2008|access-date=18 September 2009|last=Baxtor|first=Trevor}}{{Cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7736947.stm |title=Rugby League World Cup final 2008|publisher=[[British Broadcasting Corporation|BBC]]|date=22 November 2008|access-date=18 September 2009|last=Fletcher|first=Paul}} Wigan played three [[Exhibition game|friendly matches]] after the World Cup, the first being a 4–44 victory over Warrington Wolves at the [[Halliwell Jones Stadium]] during the traditional [[Christmas season|festive season]], in which Riddell and [[Karl Pryce]] both made their first-team debuts.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=3548&type=news|title=Warriors Festive Cheer|publisher=[[Wigan Warriors]]|date=28 December 2008|access-date=18 September 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110718044345/http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=3548&type=news|archive-date=18 July 2011|url-status=dead}} The Warriors played in a changed pink and black-hooped [[Kit (football)|jersey]] in support of a local [[hospice]], as [[Sam Tomkins]] completed a twenty-minute hat-trick, [[Richard Mathers]] and Pryce scored a double each, and [[Shaun Ainscough]] and [[Joel Tomkins]] also picked up one try apiece. The next match against Huddersfield Giants proved to be more difficult, as Carmont scored a last-minute try to hand Wigan a narrow victory at the [[Galpharm Stadium]], with Riddell scoring two tries and Fielden being sin-binned in the first half.{{Cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_4848478,00.html|title=Warriors edge out Giants|publisher=[[Sky Sports]]|date=25 January 2009|access-date=18 September 2009}} On the official opening on the new [[Leigh Sports Village]] and a week before the [[Super League XIV|new Super League season]] began, the Warriors whitewashed their local rivals [[Leigh Centurions]] by 0–32 with a squad in which both new signings, Roberts and Riddell, were left out of.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Warriors-ease-past-Leigh.4932724.jp |title=Warriors ease past Leigh |publisher=Wigan Today |date=30 January 2009 |access-date=18 September 2009 |last=Wilkinson |first=Phil |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120312073630/http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Warriors-ease-past-Leigh.4932724.jp |archive-date=12 March 2012 |url-status=live}}{{-}} {| class="wikitable" border="1" ! colspan="2" | LEGEND |- | style="background:#ddffdd;" width=20 |   | width=100 | Win |- | style="background:#ffffdd" width=20 |   | width=100 | Draw |- | style="background:#ffdddd;" width=20 |   | width=100 | Loss |- |} {| class="wikitable" style="font-size:95%;" width="50%" ! colspan=2 | WIGAN 6–12 Wakefield |- | {| border="0" width="100%" | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Tries:''' [[Joel Tomkins|J Tomkins]] | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Tries:''' [[Dave Halley|D Halley]] (2) |- | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Goals:''' [[Pat Richards|P Richards]] | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Goals:''' [[Tony Martin (rugby league)|T Martin]] (2) |} |} |} Wigan's season began on 8 February with a home defeat to a depleted [[Wakefield Trinity|Wakefield Trinity Wildcats]] side which only had seventeen players to fill their squad with due to injuries, and the death of [[Adam Watene]] during the off-season.{{Cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12196/4905918/wounded-cats-stun-warriors|title=Wounded Cats stun Warriors|website=Sky Sports}} Head coach [[Brian Noble (rugby league)|Brian Noble]] blamed the result on a poor completion rate of 40%. Wakefield took an early lead after [[Dave Halley]] crossed the line in the first minute, before scoring again via the same player in the fourteenth minute to make a 0–12 initiative, following two successful conversions by [[Tony Martin (rugby league)|Tony Martin]]. [[Joel Tomkins]] was introduced into the game in the second half as he scored a try which was converted by [[Pat Richards]], but Wigan failed to score again. [[Richard Mathers]], who was [[Ejection (sports)|sent-off]] twice in 2008,{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Mathers-in-the-dock.4961241.jp |title=Mathers in the dock |publisher=Wigan Today |date=9 February 2009 |access-date=18 September 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090418062538/http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Mathers-in-the-dock.4961241.jp |archive-date=18 April 2009 |url-status=live}} was put into the [[sin-bin]] by the referee, Richard Silverwood, for the deliberate obstruction of Halley, and match ended in the first defeat for Wigan in their 2009 Lancashire|Preston]] area, in exchange for the student team at the university displaying the Wigan Warriors logo.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=3647&type=news|title=Warriors and UCLan in Exciting Partnership|publisher=[[Wigan Warriors]]|date=10 February 2009|access-date=18 September 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110718044405/http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=3647&type=news|archive-date=18 July 2011|url-status=dead}} This news was announced on the same day Mathers learned he would miss the next two matches for tripping Halley,{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Twomatch-ban-for-Mathers.4967803.jp|title=Two match ban for Mathers|publisher=Wigan Today|date=11 February 2009|access-date=18 September 2009|last=Dewhurst|first=Tony}} including the club's next match against [[Hull F.C.]] which they lost at the [[KC Stadium]].{{Cite news|url=http://www.timesonline.co.uk/tol/sport/rugby_league/article5729470.ece|title=Peter Cusack seal the deal for Hull|work=[[The Times]]|date=14 February 2009|access-date=18 September 2009|last=Irvine|first=Christopher | location=London}}{{dead link|date=September 2024|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}} Hull FC started strongly, but Wigan scored first off a [[Tim Smith (rugby league)|Tim Smith]] kick to [[Darrell Goulding|Goulding]] and [[Cameron Phelps|Phelps]] which eventually resulted in a try for [[Phil Bailey|Bailey]]. [[Richard Horne]] scored for Hull to level the scores at 6–6 before a last resort intervention from [[Kirk Yeaman]] denied Goulding a try. The scores were therefore level at half-time, and a kick from Smith resulted in another try for Richards who bettered his opposite winger, [[Mark Calderwood]] in the air. With four key players missing—[[Mark Riddell|Riddell]], Phelps, Bailey and Richards—due to injury, Wigan tired in the final quarter allowing [[Chris Thorman]] and [[Peter Cusack (rugby league)|Peter Cusack]] to score late in the game to put the tie beyond the visiting team. {{-}} {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#D31718; color:#FFFFFF;"| Round 2 – Castleford Tigers |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 20 February :'''Venue:''' [[JJB Stadium]] :'''Referee:''' S Ganson {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 6–16 :'''Attendance:''' 12,079 :'''[http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3086255 RFL report]''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | WIGAN 22–28 Castleford |- | {| border="0" width="100%" | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Tries:''' [[Karl Pryce|K Pryce]], [[Darrell Goulding|D Goulding]], [[Shaun Ainscough|S Ainscough]], [[Thomas Leuluai|T Leuluai]], [[Joel Tomkins|J Tomkins]] | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Tries:''' [[Michael Shenton|M Shenton]], [[Kirk Dixon|K Dixon]], [[Brett Ferres|B Ferres]] (2), [[Joe Westerman|J Westerman]] |- | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Goals:''' [[Amos Roberts|A Roberts]] | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Goals:''' K Dixon (4) |} |} |} With Mathers suspended and Cameron Phelps injured with a [[hamstring]] injury and two other injury concerns, [[Karl Pryce]] and [[Shaun Ainscough]] were given places in the initial 19-man squad to take on [[Castleford Tigers]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=3649&type=news|title=Noble announces 19 for Tiger test|publisher=[[Wigan Warriors]]|date=18 February 2009|access-date=18 September 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110718044426/http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=3649&type=news|archive-date=18 July 2011|url-status=dead}} A third defeat out of three matches came as Castleford won a ten try game 22–28, a result which meant Wigan's worst start to a season in twenty-four years.{{Cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/4740897/Wigan-mark-their-worst-start-in-24-years-against-Castleford.html|title=Wigan mark their worst start in 24 years against Castleford|work=[[The Daily Telegraph|The Telegraph]]|date=21 February 2009|access-date=18 September 2009|last=Fisher|first=Michael | location=London}} With Richards still missing due to injury, conversions proved to be the difference as both teams scored five tries apiece, but kickers Roberts and Riddell could only manage one successful kick whilst the Tigers' kicker [[Kirk Dixon]] managed four. Two disallowed Castleford tries in the first ten minutes were followed by two penalty goals by Dixon to give Castleford a 0–4 lead. [[Thomas Leuluai]]'s effort gave Wigan a try before [[Joe Westerman]] put the Tigers ahead again after 34 minutes. Castleford scored again through [[Brett Ferres]], with Dixon converting to give Castleford a 6–16 lead at half-time. Wigan scored first after the break thanks to a try from [[Karl Pryce]], but this was unconverted and Castleford replied with two more of their own with Dixon and Ferres scoring his second try. Wigan started a fight back, initially through a Goulding try, and then again through Joel Tomkins. Ainscough marked his debut with a fifty-yard try to reduce the deficit to two points, but [[Michael Shenton]]'s try sealed the victory for Castleford. [[Michael McIlorum]] was also cited and suspended by the [[Rugby Football League]] after the game for a dangerous throw on [[Mitchell Sargent]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.sportinglife.com/rugbyleague/news/story_get.cgi?STORY_NAME=rleague/09/02/24/RUGBYL_Disciplinary.html |title=Warriors hooker banned |publisher=Sporting Life |access-date=18 September 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110605031703/http://www.sportinglife.com/rugbyleague/news/story_get.cgi?STORY_NAME=rleague/09/02/24/RUGBYL_Disciplinary.html |archive-date=5 June 2011 |url-status=live}} After failing to gain a point from the first three matches in the season, Noble's position as head coach was strengthened when [[Ian Lenagan]] publicly supported him, stating, "Brian is a very experienced coach and I'm sure we'll get this right. He has had bad starts to the season before, and he has good starts."{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Lenagan--Nobby-safe.5013441.jp |title=Lenagan – Nobby safe |publisher=Wigan Today |date=25 February 2009 |access-date=18 September 2009 |last=Dewhurst |first=Tony |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090317004844/http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Lenagan--Nobby-safe.5013441.jp |archive-date=17 March 2009 |url-status=live}} {{-}} {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#D31718; color:#FFFFFF;"| Round 3 – Harlequins Rugby League |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 28 February :'''Venue:''' [[The Stoop|Twickenham Stoop]] :'''Referee:''' T Alibert {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 10–6 :'''Attendance:''' 3,883 :'''[http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3086266 RFL report]''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | Harlequins RL 18–24 WIGAN |- | {| border="0" width="100%" | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Tries:''' [[Chris Melling (rugby league)|C Melling]], [[Matt Gafa|M Gafa]], [[Chad Randall|C Randall]], [[Louis McCarthy-Scarsbrook|L McCarthy-Scarsbrook]] | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Tries:''' [[Amos Roberts|A Roberts]], [[Phil Bailey|P Bailey]], [[George Carmont|G Carmont]], [[Shaun Ainscough|S Ainscough]] (2) |- | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Goals:''' [[Danny Orr|D Orr]] | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Goals:''' [[Pat Richards|P Richards]] (2) |} |} |} Wigan recorded their first win of the season with an 18–24 victory over [[London Broncos#2006–2011: Harlequins Rugby League|Harlequins RL]] at the [[The Stoop|Twickenham Stoop]] in London.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Warriors-claim-first-Super-League.5026660.jp |title=Warriors claim first Super League win |publisher=Wigan Today |date=28 February 2009 |access-date=18 September 2009 |last=Wilkinson |first=Phil |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090316002522/http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Warriors-claim-first-Super-League.5026660.jp |archive-date=16 March 2009 |url-status=live}} Ainscough scored his first of two tries after a break from Roberts down the wing, and it was converted by Richards who was playing at [[rugby league positions#Fullback|fullback]] as a replacement for Mathers who was omitted from the squad. [[Chad Randall]] punished Joel Tomkins' handling error for Harlequins' first try before [[Matt Gafa]] scored another try for the home side to make the half-time score 10–6. On his Super League debut, [[Sam Tomkins]]—Joel's younger brother—was involved in the build-up to Carmont's try after the second half had begun, and Bailey scored another try to put Wigan 10–14 ahead following two missed conversion attempts. A disallowed try for a knock-on by [[Will Sharp]] was followed by a knock-on from Carmont, before a [[40-20]] gave [[Louis McCarthy-Scarsbrook]] a chance to score a try in the 64th minute. [[Chris Melling (rugby league)|Chris Melling]] restored a four-point lead for the home side with ten minutes left in the game after [[Harrison Hansen]] knocked-on. In the 75th minute, Randall's pass was intercepted by Roberts for an easy intercept try which Richards converted to give a two-point lead, which was increased to six as Ainscough scored the final try of the game. ===March=== {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#D31718; color:#FFFFFF;"| Round 4 – Bradford Bulls |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 7 March :'''Venue:''' [[JJB Stadium]] :'''Referee:''' B Thaler {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 18–0 :'''Attendance:''' 12,588 :'''[http://superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3115378 RFL report]''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | WIGAN 44–10 Bradford |- | '''Tries:''' [[Pat Richards|P Richards]]
[[George Carmont|G Carmont]]
[[Iafeta Palea'aesina|I Palea'aesina]]
[[Gareth Hock|G Hock]] (2)
[[Harrison Hansen|H Hansen]] (2)
'''Goals:''' [[Pat Richards|P Richards]] (8) | '''Tries:''' [[Rikki Sheriffe|R Sheriffe]]
[[Steve Menzies|S Menzies]]
'''Goals:''' [[Paul Deacon|P Deacon]] |} |} The start of March was also the start of the reserve team's season, which begin with a comfortable win against [[Bradford Bulls]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=3717&type=news|title=Warriors Reserves Defeat Bulls|publisher=[[Wigan Warriors]]|date=7 March 2009|access-date=21 September 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110718044436/http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=3717&type=news|archive-date=18 July 2011|url-status=dead}} Richards scored twenty points including eight goals and a try as the first team earned their first home victory of the season, winning by a margin of thirty-four points.{{Cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_report/0,19936,11070_3115378,00.html|title=Warriors crush tame Bulls|publisher=[[Sky Sports]]|date=8 March 2009|access-date=21 September 2009}} Richards scored the first of seven Wigan tries after nine minutes, before Hock broke the defensive line to put Wigan 12–0 ahead. Another line break from Hock provided an offload to Carmont who scored a third try. A twenty-point gap emerged as Richards kicked a penalty conceded by [[Paul Sykes (rugby league)|Paul Sykes]], before Bradford scored their first of two tries through [[Rikki Sheriffe]] after 48 minutes. Hock replied by scoring his second try, before a line break by Riddell handed a try to [[Harrison Hansen]]. [[Wayne Godwin]] broke his leg after stopping an [[Iafeta Palea'aesina]] run, before Palea'aesina himself scored Wigan's final try. [[Steve Menzies]] scored his first Bradford try before the match ended. {{-}} {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#D31718; color:#FFFFFF;"| Round 5 – Leeds Rhinos |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 13 March :'''Venue:''' [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley Stadium]] :'''Referee:''' I Smith {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 22–6 :'''Attendance:''' 17,677 :'''[http://superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3086276 RFL report] ''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | Leeds 34–10 WIGAN |- | '''Tries:''' [[Scott Donald|S Donald]]
[[Ryan Hall (rugby league)|R Hall]] (2)
[[Rob Burrow|R Burrow]]
[[Kylie Leuluai|K Leuluai]]
[[Jamie Peacock|J Peacock]]
'''Goals:''' [[Kevin Sinfield|K Sinfield]] (5) | '''Tries:''' [[Shaun Ainscough|S Ainscough]]
[[Gareth Hock|G Hock]]
'''Goals:''' [[Pat Richards|P Richards]] |} |} Wigan's run of victories was short-lived as they lost the away tie to [[Leeds Rhinos]] immediately after. An unchanged Wigan side from the tie against Bradford could only score two tries, conceding six the other way.{{Cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7928232.stm|title=Leeds 34–10 Wigan|publisher=[[British Broadcasting Corporation|BBC]]|date=13 March 2009|access-date=21 September 2009}} The victory was the Rhinos' fifth out of five matches, and they were ahead after three minutes thanks to a 40–20 which was converted into a try by [[Kylie Leuluai]]. An error from Ainscough proved costly as [[Scott Donald]] scored from the resulting turnover. Upon the half-hour mark, Leeds were 22–0 ahead after [[Rob Burrow]] touched down under the sticks and another 40–20 provided a try, this time for [[Ryan Hall (rugby league)|Ryan Hall]]. Ainscough scored Wigan's first try following a pass from Thomas Leuluai, before Wigan were disallowed another try after the video referee Phil Bentham ruled [[Sean O'Loughlin]] had obstructed Burrow. Hall scored his second, besting Roberts in the air, after fifty minutes. Peacock scored Leeds' sixth try, bursting through the defensive line, whilst Hock scored the last try of the game. {{-}} {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#D31718; color:#FFFFFF;"| Round 6 – Salford City Reds |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 20 March :'''Venue:''' [[The Willows, Salford|The Willows]] :'''Referee:''' G Hewer {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 6–22 :'''Attendance:''' 7,016 :'''[http://superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3086284 RFL report] ''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | Salford 12–38 WIGAN |- | '''Tries:''' [[Richie Myler|R Myler]] (2)

'''Goals:''' [[Pat Richards|P Richards]] (5) |} |} An away tie to [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]] was more favourable for Wigan as they eased to victory at [[The Willows, Salford|The Willows]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.sportinglife.com/rugbyleague/reports/story_get.cgi?STORY_NAME=rleague/09/03/20/RUGBYL_Salford.html |title=Salford 12 Wigan 38 |publisher=Sporting Life |date=20 March 2009 |access-date=21 September 2009 |last=Hampson |first=Andy |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110605031857/http://www.sportinglife.com/rugbyleague/reports/story_get.cgi?STORY_NAME=rleague/09/03/20/RUGBYL_Salford.html |archive-date=5 June 2011 |url-status=live}} Salford had the first major chance of the game as a kick from the 18-year-old [[Richie Myler]] was knocked-on by [[Jeremy Smith (rugby league born 1981)|Jeremy Smith]]. Carmont replied, catching an offload from former-Salford player [[Andy Coley]], to hand a try to Ainscough. Myler again created an opportunity, but [[Karl Fitzpatrick]] was pushed into touch. A break via Ainscough and Riddell gave Leuluai a try, before Myler scored Salford's first try following a kick from Jeremy Smith. Sam Tomkins saw a hole in the defensive line and ran through it, scoring Wigan's third try, before Richards scored a fourth to make the half-time score 22–6. Wigan scored first again in the second half as Sam Tomkins scored from the half-way line, before Ainscough scored his second. Myler and Jeremy Smith again combined for the former's second try, this time from a pass, but Richards sealed the game for Wigan after offloading to Leuluai for his second and Wigan's seventh try. The win was dampened by the injury of [[Ireland national rugby league team|Ireland]] international [[Eamon O'Carroll]], who fractured his [[metatarsal]] bone during the reserve game against Salford.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Bad-break-for-O39Carroll.5101591.jp|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130222101446/http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Bad-break-for-O39Carroll.5101591.jp|url-status=dead|archive-date=22 February 2013|title=Bad break for O'Carroll|publisher=Wigan Today|date=24 March 2009|access-date=21 September 2009|last=Dewhurst|first=Tony}} {{-}} {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#D31718; color:#FFFFFF;"| Round 7 – Huddersfield Giants |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 27 March :'''Venue:''' [[JJB Stadium]] :'''Referee:''' P Bentham {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 4–10 :'''Attendance:''' 11,670 :'''[http://superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3086291 RFL report] ''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | WIGAN 8–22 Huddersfield |- | '''Tries:''' [[Amos Roberts|A Roberts]]
[[Phil Bailey|P Bailey]] | '''Tries:''' [[Michael Lawrence (rugby league)|M Lawrence]]
[[Kevin Brown (English rugby league)|K Brown]]
[[Scott Moore (rugby league)|S Moore]]
'''Goals:''' [[Brett Hodgson|B Hodgson]] (5) |} |} In a game that was 8–10 going into the final ten minutes, and had three players sent to the sin-bin, Wigan lost their third home game. Amos Roberts returned after being dropped to the reserves the week before, whilst the visiting Huddersfield team contained five former Wigan players. [[Brett Hodgson]] gave Huddersfield the initiative by kicking a penalty for a 0–2 lead, and Hock almost responded but knocked-on in the act of scoring a try. Bailey eventually scored a try for Wigan, but Richards failed to convert, and the score was made level after another kicked penalty from Brett Hodgson. [[Scott Moore (rugby league)|Scott Moore]] gave Huddersfield the lead again after a run from dummy half to give a 4–10 lead, despite a take-out on Leuluai just before half-time which went unpenalised. [[Luke Robinson (rugby league)|Luke Robinson]] was sin-binned for delaying play and Roberts almost made the advantage count, but grounded the ball in touch and the try was disallowed. [[Darrell Griffin]] and Hansen were then sin-binned for a fight, before passing along the backs lead to a Roberts try, but Richards again failed to convert. Ainscough then fumbled a kick from Robinson, and [[Michael Lawrence (rugby league)|Michael Lawrence]] punished the mistake by scoring a try for Huddersfield. Brown scored minutes later to put the game beyond Wigan. ===April=== {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#790363; color:#FFFFFF;"| Challenge Cup round 4 – Barrow Raiders |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 5 April :'''Venue:''' [[Craven Park (Barrow)|Craven Park]] :'''Referee:''' J Leahy {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 14–20 :'''Attendance:''' 6,275 :'''[https://web.archive.org/web/20120224014654/http://www.therfl.co.uk/~therflc/results/report.php?z=challengecup&id=3121084 RFL report]''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | Barrow 20–32 WIGAN |- | '''Tries:''' [[James Finch (rugby league)|J Finch]]

'''Goals:''' [[Mark Riddell|M Riddell]]
[[Amos Roberts|A Roberts]] |} |} The fourth round of the [[2009 Challenge Cup]] occupied the first weekend of April, and for all Super League clubs this meant their first Challenge Cup ties. In March, Wigan learned that they would face an away tie to the [[Barrow Raiders]], and it was expected this match would be held in front of the first sell-out crowd at [[Barrow-in-Furness|Barrow]]'s [[Craven Park (Barrow)|Craven Park]] for twenty-five years.{{Cite web |url=http://www.nwemail.co.uk/raiders/news/des_delighted_as_raiders_land_wigan_plum_1_524381?referrerPath=home |title=Des delighted as Raiders land Wigan plum |publisher=[[North-West Evening Mail]] |date=10 March 2009 |access-date=22 September 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090318011509/http://www.nwemail.co.uk/raiders/news/des_delighted_as_raiders_land_wigan_plum_1_524381?referrerPath=home |archive-date=18 March 2009 |url-status=dead}}{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Lenagan-Cumbria-deserves-Super-League.5133856.jp|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130222210453/http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Lenagan-Cumbria-deserves-Super-League.5133856.jp|url-status=dead|archive-date=22 February 2013|title=Cumbria deserves SL team|publisher=Wigan Today|date=2 April 2009|access-date=22 September 2009|last=Wilkinson|first=Phil}} The game itself was still in the balance fifteen minutes from time, with Barrow trailing Wigan by four points at 20–24, following a match which had seen a Shaun Ainscough hat-trick and Wigan's defensive frailties exploited again.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Warriors-edge-into-fifth-round.5141180.jp|title=Barrow 20 Warriors 34|publisher=Wigan Today|date=6 April 2009|access-date=22 September 2009|last=Wilkinson|first=Phil}} The result pivoted on a forward pass decision which ruled out another Barrow try, minutes before Pryce scored a controversial try for Wigan in which he appeared to have knocked-on, with twelve minutes left to play. A try from Tomkins eventually sealed the win for Wigan to advance them into the fifth round of the competition. {{-}} {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#D31718; color:#FFFFFF;"| Round 8 – St Helens RLFC |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 9 April :'''Venue:''' [[JJB Stadium]] :'''Referee:''' B Thaler {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 6–6 :'''Attendance:''' 22,232 :'''[http://superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3086298 RFL report]''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | WIGAN 12–19 St Helens |- | '''Tries:''' [[Shaun Ainscough|S Ainscough]] (2)
'''Goals:''' [[Amos Roberts|A Roberts]] (2) | '''Tries:''' [[Matt Gidley|M Gidley]]
[[Leon Pryce|L Pryce]]
[[Keiron Cunningham|K Cunningham]]
'''Goals:''' [[Paul Wellens|P Wellens]]
[[Kyle Eastmond|K Eastmond]] (2)
'''Drops:''' [[Kyle Eastmond|K Eastmond]] |} |} On the day after, it was confirmed [[Martin Gleeson (rugby league)|Martin Gleeson]] would transfer to Wigan from the [[Warrington Wolves]], on a three-and-a-half-year contract.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=4288&type=news|title=Wigan Warriors Sign Martin Gleeson|date=6 April 2009|publisher=[[Wigan Warriors]]|access-date=23 October 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110718044458/http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=4288&type=news|archive-date=18 July 2011|url-status=dead}} The signing came after Warrington had left Gleeson out of the squad which defeated [[York City Knights]] in their fourth round Cup tie, meaning Gleeson would be eligible to play for Wigan in future Cup games.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Glee-for-Warriors.5144001.jp|title=Glee for Warriors|date=6 April 2009|publisher=Wigan Today|access-date=23 October 2009|first=Tony|last=Dewhurst}} Wigan were reported by the [[Wigan Evening Post]] to have spent [[Pound sterling|£]]110,000 and given [[Richie Mathers]] in return for Gleeson.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Gleeson-I39ve-come-home.5148092.jp|title=Gleeson: I've come home|date=7 April 2009|publisher=Wigan Today|access-date=23 October 2009|first=Tony|last=Dewhurst}} On the day after Gleeson's signing was announced, the draw for the Challenge Cup's fifth round took place. Wigan received an away tie to [[Wakefield Trinity|Wakefield Trinity Wildcats]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=3736&type=news|title=Warriors Away at Wakefield in Challenge Cup|date=7 April 2009|publisher=[[Wigan Warriors]]|access-date=23 October 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110718044549/http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=3736&type=news|archive-date=18 July 2011|url-status=dead}} Gleeson featured in the first derby match of the year against St Helens RLFC. Ainscough put the home Wigan side ahead after nine minutes with a try on the wing, following a kick from Leuluai.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Warriors-lose-against-Saints.5160953.jp|title=Wigan push Saints all the way|publisher=Wigan Today|date=9 April 2009|access-date=29 December 2009|first=Phil|last=Wilkinson}} Richards missed the conversion, and so Wigan only went four points ahead before St Helens replied with a try of their own through [[Keiron Cunningham]], with [[Paul Wellens]] making the conversion. Hock had a try disallowed for a double movement, and later on a high tackle on Paleaaesina gave Roberts an opportunity to level the scores, with which he succeeded to draw the score at 6–6 at half time. Ainscough again carved out a lead for Wigan in the second half, and Roberts converted from the sideline. Two quick St Helens tries through [[Matt Gidley]] and [[Leon Pryce]] gave the visitors a 12–18 lead, and a [[Kyle Eastmond]] drop goal sealed the match for them. Prior to their next match, Barrow Raiders confirmed that they had signed [[Lee Mossop]] on a month-long loan. {{-}} {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#D31718; color:#FFFFFF;"| Round 9 – Catalans Dragons |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 13 April :'''Venue:''' [[Stade Gilbert Brutus]] :'''Referee:''' P Bentham {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 22–14 :'''Attendance:''' 9,490 :'''[http://superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3086304 RFL report]''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | Catalans 40–24 WIGAN |- | '''Tries:''' [[Clint Greenshields|C Greenshields]] (2)

[[Sam Tomkins|S Tomkins]]
'''Goals:''' [[Pat Richards|P Richards]] (2) |} |} Despite providing a close fight against St Helens, the club's visit to [[Perpignan]] for their away tie against [[Catalans Dragons]] ended in its third consecutive loss. The Dragons scored first through [[Dimitri Pelo]]'s try in the second minute.{{Cite web|url=http://www.sportinglife.com/rugbyleague/reports/story_get.cgi?STORY_NAME=rleague/09/04/13/RUGBYL_Catalans_Nightlead.html|title=Catalans Dragons 40 Wigan 24|publisher=Sporting Life|date=13 April 2009|access-date=30 December 2009}} Wigan scored fourteen points in return, with three tries from Roberts, Ainscough and Gleeson and one conversion for a 4–14 lead. A ten-point lead proved precarious as Catalans ran in thirty-six unanswered points, the first of them coming through a [[Greg Bird (rugby league)|Greg Bird]] try. [[Grégory Mounis]] scored next, and [[Thomas Bosc]] kicked the conversion to give the home side the lead, and [[Jason Ryles]] gained another try before half-time. The Catalans fullback [[Clint Greenshields]] scored first after the break and also scored the next try. Ryles also scored a second try of his own to open a twenty-six-point deficit. Tomkins and Richards gave Wigan two late tries, but the match by then was all but finished. Similarly to Mossop, [[Darrell Goulding]] completed a transfer to Salford City Reds on a month-long loan deal. After an extension to this deal in May, Goulding was to be eventually recalled on 7 July to cope with an injury crisis. {{-}} {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#D31718; color:#FFFFFF;"| Round 10 – Celtic Crusaders |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 19 April :'''Venue:''' [[JJB Stadium]] :'''Referee:''' S Ganson {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 20–4 :'''Attendance:''' 12,371 :'''[http://superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3086314 RFL report]''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | WIGAN 44–10 Celtic |- | '''Tries:''' [[Amos Roberts|A Roberts]]

'''Goals:''' [[Jace Van Dijk|J Van Dijk]] |} |} The club's next match was against newly promoted [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders]], who had not won a match yet in the Super League season coming into this fixture.{{Cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_report/0,19936,11070_3086314,00.html|title=Wigan enjoy last Crusaders|publisher=[[Sky Sports]]|date=19 April 2009|access-date=21 January 2010}} Celtic led after two minutes, when [[Luke Dyer]] pounced for a try after Phelps mistakenly let the ball bounce into the in-goal area. Richards replied with a try on the wing, before O'Loughlin gathered his own kick for a solo try to present a lead to the home Wigan side. Two more tries came for Wigan before half-time with Riddell and Roberts both scoring one each. Sam Tomkins scored not long after the break thanks to a run from Palea'asesina. Carmont stretched the lead further with a try of his own, and O'Loughlin scored his second try of the match. [[Damien Quinn (rugby league)|Damien Quinn]] was sin-binned in his 100th competitive appearance for the Crusaders—[[Marshall Chalk]] was also sin-binned later on.{{Cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2009/apr/20/celtic-crusaders-wigan-warriors-super-league|title=Celtic Crusaders crushed by eight-try Wigan as winless newcomers flounder|work=[[The Guardian]]|date=20 April 2009|access-date=21 January 2009|first=Gareth|last=Walker | location=London}} Richards claimed his second try almost instantly after Quinn's dismissal. [[Mark Dalle Cort]] scored a late Celtic try and captain [[Jace Van Dijk]] successfully converted, but Wigan had another try before the game ended, with Sam Tomkins grounding his second try of the match—his team's ninth—to complete a convincing victory for the home side. Transfer news occupied the break between this win and the Warriors' second league match against Wakefield Trinity Wildcats. The [[Wigan Evening Post]] reported that the club, as well as Leeds Rhinos, were interested in signing Richie Myler from Salford City Reds,{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Warriors-keep-eye-on-Myler.5193749.jp|title=Warriors keep eye on Myler|publisher=Wigan Today|date=22 April 2009|access-date=21 January 2010}} although neither club were successful in their approaches to the player. Warrington Wolves would eventually sign Myler later in the year.{{Cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/6147280/Richie-Myler-heads-list-of-Warrington-signings.html|title=Richie Myler heads list of Warrington signings|date=6 September 2009|access-date=21 January 2010|first=Michael|last=Fisher | location=London | work=The Daily Telegraph}} Meanwhile, Thomas Leuluai extended his contract with the club for a further three years.{{Cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2009/apr/22/hull-kr-coach-justin-morgan-british-record-fine|title=Hull KR coach Justin Morgan handed record British fine|work=[[The Guardian]]|date=22 April 2009|access-date=21 January 2010|first=Andy|last=Wilson | location=London}} {{-}} {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#D31718; color:#FFFFFF;"| Round 11 – Wakefield Trinity Wildcats |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 26 April :'''Venue:''' [[Belle Vue (Wakefield)|Belle Vue]] :'''Referee:''' B Thaler {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 20–22 :'''Attendance:''' 5,521 :'''[http://superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3086324 RFL report]''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | Wakefield 26–40 WIGAN |- | '''Tries:''' [[Damien Blanch|D Blanch]] (2)

'''Goals:''' [[Pat Richards|P Richards]] (6) |} |} George Carmont had to be replaced at short notice prior to kick-off against the club's next opponents, Wakefield Trinity Wildcats. Shaun Ainscough was recalled to take his place in the team, and scored four tries to give his side a second consecutive win.{{Cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_report/0,19936,11070_3086324,00.html|title=Ainscough stars in Wigan win|publisher=[[Sky Sports]]|date=24 April 2009|access-date=22 January 2010}} Wakefield, like Celtic, put the first points on the scoreboard after Damien Blanch received a kick from Danny Brough for a try on the wing, with Brough converting for a 6–0 lead. Tim Smith linked with Cameron Phelps, who was switched from fullback to center in Carmont's absence, for Wigan's first try. Phelps turned provider as his work gave Ainscough two tries in nine minutes for a 6–16 lead, before Wigan's indiscipline gave Brough a penalty and a try following a handling error from the stand-in fullback, Pat Richards. Ainscough scored again on the wing to complete his hat-trick, but Wakefield replied after Brough intercepted a pass from Martin Gleeson and returned 45 metres for his second try to make the scores 20–22 at half time. Wakefield took the lead shortly after the break as Blanch took advantage of Ainscough's weak defence for his second try. This was Wakefield's last try of the match, as Phelps gathered a kick from Smith to score a try next to the posts which Richards converted successfully.{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Ainscough-grabs-four-tries-against.5204850.jp|title=Ainscough grabs four|publisher=Wigan Today|date=24 April 2009|access-date=21 January 2010|first=Phil|last=Wilkinson}} Riddell was injured with a hamstring, and despite Wakefield pressure, Wigan scored again as a run from Sam Tomkins led to another try for Ainscough—his sixteenth in ten matches. Gleeson scored in the final ten minutes to put the game beyond the home side's reach. ===May=== {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#D31718; color:#FFFFFF;"| Round 12 – St Helens RLFC |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 2 May :'''Venue:''' [[Murrayfield Stadium]] :'''Referee:''' S Ganson {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 24–6 :'''Attendance:''' 29,627 :'''[http://superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3086331 RFL report]''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | WIGAN 38–18 St Helens |- | '''Tries:''' [[Pat Richards|P Richards]] (2)

[[Kyle Eastmond|K Eastmond]] |} |} Ahead of their 2009 [[Magic Weekend]] fixture against St Helens RLFC, [[Thomas Leuluai]] learned that he would not be called up to play in the 2009 [[ANZAC Test]], news which was welcomed by Brian Noble, who stated that New Zealand had, "enough southern hemisphere-based Kiwis to cope."{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Noble-delight-at-Leuluai-Test.5226232.jp|title=Noble delight at Leuluai Test snub|publisher=Wigan Today|date=1 May 2009|access-date=22 January 2010|first=Tony|last=Dewhurst}} Leuluai instead took membership of a Wigan side which recorded its third successive league win at [[Murrayfield Stadium]] over a St Helens RLFC side including [[James Graham (rugby league)|James Graham]], who had complained earlier in the week about the event's location being too far away.{{Cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/tv_show/story/0,20144,12387_5256446_12387,00.html|title=Let's give Scots a try|publisher=[[Sky Sports]]|date=29 April 2009|access-date=22 January 2010}} Wigan had lost to St Helens at the Magic Weekend event in 2007 and 2008, 34–18 and 57–16 respectively, but it was Wigan's captain [[Sean O'Loughlin]] who scored the game's first try after seven minutes.{{Cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2009/may/02/st-helens-wigan-super-league|title=Brilliant Wigan produce Edinburgh magic to beat St Helens|work=[[The Guardian]]|date=2 May 2009|access-date=22 January 2010| location=London}} Pat Richards scored the match's second try following a kick from O'Loughlin, and added both conversions to give Wigan a 12–0 lead. This was further extended after [[Gareth Hock]] scored after 29 minutes. [[Sean Long]] gained a try back for St Helens as his grubber kick rebounded off Leuluai, before a break from Sam Tomkins fed his older brother Joel for a try just before half time. Two minutes after the break, Wigan kicked a penalty to stretch the lead to 26–6, and Wigan ended any hope of a St Helens comeback as Leuluai scored following a break down the wing from Richards. St Helens scored the next two tries through [[Leon Pryce]] and [[Paul Wellens]], with Long kicking both conversions, but Richards scored his second try and kicked the conversion to take his personal haul to twenty-two, and end the match 38–18. With Noble's contract due to expire at the end of the season, [[Ian Lenagan]] praised him for Wigan's biggest derby win in six years over St Helens, but refused to offer any assurances about Noble's future at the club, after [[Bradford Bulls]] refuted a claim that they wanted to replace [[Steve McNamara]] with Noble.{{Cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_5274075,00.html|title=Wigan chairman praises Noble|publisher=[[Sky Sports]]|date=3 May 2009|access-date=22 January 2010}} {{-}} {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#790363; color:#FFFFFF;"| Challenge Cup round 5 – Wakefield Trinity Wildcats |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 9 May :'''Venue:''' [[Belle Vue (Wakefield)|Belle Vue]] :'''Referee:''' P Bentham {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 11–16 :'''Attendance:''' 4,883 :'''[https://web.archive.org/web/20120308104817/http://www.therfl.co.uk/~therflc/results/report.php?z=challengecup&id=3127848 RFL report]''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | Wakefield 17–28 WIGAN |- | '''Tries:''' [[Jason Demetriou (rugby league)|J Demetriou]]

'''Goals:''' [[Pat Richards|P Richards]] (4) |} |} For the next match, the players again returned to [[Belle Vue (Wakefield)|Belle Vue]], two weeks after their league win. The Wakefield Trinity Wildcats were coached by [[John Kear]], twice a winner of the Cup as a head coach, most recently in [[2005 Challenge Cup|2005]] with [[Hull F.C.|Hull FC]],{{Cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_5285570,00.html|title=Atkins hails Mr Motivator|publisher=[[Sky Sports]]|date=5 May 2009|access-date=22 January 2010}} and in [[1998 Challenge Cup|1998]] when his [[Sheffield Eagles]] side defeated Wigan 17–8 in the final, three years after Wigan's last of a record eight consecutive Challenge Cup final victories.{{Cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/challenge_cup/4158148.stm|title=Roll of honour|publisher=BBC|date=26 August 2006|access-date=23 February 2011}} The match did not start in Wigan's favour, as [[Danny Brough]] provided passes to [[Jason Demetriou (rugby league)|Jason Demetriou]] and [[Tevita Leo-Latu]], and kicked a conversion to give the Wildcats a 10–0 lead.{{Cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/5301119/Stuart-Fielden-ends-two-year-wait-to-cap-Wigan-fightback-against-Wakefield.html|title=Stuart Fielden ends two-year wait to cap Wigan fightback against Wakefield |work=[[The Daily Telegraph]]|date=9 May 2009|access-date=22 January 2010|first=Ian|last=Laybourn | location=London}} Wigan went in ahead at half time though. Wigan got their first points with a try on the wing from Richards, and then another try from [[Michael McIlorum]] following a Gareth Hock pass. Brough kicked a drop-goal to give Wakefield a point lead, but Cameron Phelps scored a third Wigan try after Sean O'Loughlin broke the line from his own half. Wakefield went ahead for the third time twelve minutes after the break, with a grubber kick from Brough being gathered by [[Tony Martin (rugby league)|Tony Martin]]. Stuart Fielden then scored his first try in two years before Sam Tomkins finished the game with a try of his own making. The fourth goal of the match meant the final scores were 17–28; a win for Wigan which advanced them to the quarter-finals. The day after, the draw for the next round to be played three weeks later was made, with Wigan receiving a home tie against Salford City Reds.{{Cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/8042939.stm|title=Saints sent to Gateshead in Cup|publisher=[[British Broadcasting Corporation|BBC]]|date=10 May 2009|access-date=24 February 2010}} {{-}} {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#D31718; color:#FFFFFF;"| Round 13 – Hull Kingston Rovers |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 15 May :'''Venue:''' [[JJB Stadium]] :'''Referee:''' R Silverwood {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 6–6 :'''Attendance:''' 13,415 :'''[http://superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3086333 RFL report]''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | WIGAN 12–20 Hull KR |- | {| border="0" width="100%" | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Tries:''' [[Sean O'Loughlin|S O'Loughlin]] (2) | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Tries:''' [[Jake Webster|J Webster]], [[Paul Cooke (rugby league)|P Cooke]], [[Michael Dobson (rugby league)|M Dobson]] |- | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Goals:''' [[Pat Richards|P Richards]] (2) | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Goals:''' M Dobson (4) |} |} |} Following a run of four games, the club's next match was against [[Hull Kingston Rovers]]. Their preparations were boosted by news that Sam Tomkins would not receive a match ban for his conduct against Wakefield in the Challenge Cup tie.{{Cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/8042939.stm|title=Tomkins avoids ban|publisher=[[Sky Sports]]|date=12 May 2009|access-date=24 February 2010}} Goulding extended his loan spell at Salford City Reds, but with a condition that Wigan could recall him with 24 hours notice.{{Cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_5321920,00.html|title=Goulding extends Salford loan|publisher=[[Sky Sports]]|date=13 May 2009|access-date=24 February 2010}} The match seemed to be heading towards a 6–0 lead for Wigan at half-time, after Sean O'Loughlin scored a try and Richards kicked a goal, but a controversial try scored by [[Michael Dobson (rugby league)|Michael Dobson]] after the hooter had sounded was awarded by the referee, Richard Silverwood.{{Cite web|url=http://www.manchestereveningnews.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/wigan-12-hull-kr-20-919739|title=Wigan 12 Hull KR 20|date=15 May 2009|website=Manchester Evening News}} Incensed, Wigan started the second half with another converted try from the captain, O'Loughlin, but this was to be their final score of the match. [[Paul Cooke (rugby league)|Paul Cooke]] collected his own kick for a solo try, and [[Jake Webster]] put Hull KR into the lead, with Dobson kicking both goals for a six-point advantage. A late penalty was kicked by Dobson for another two ponts, concluding the match in favour of Hull KR. The match ended Wigan's run of four victories, and placed Hull KR temporarily at the top of the Super League XIV table. {{-}} {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#D31718; color:#FFFFFF;"| Round 14 – Warrington Wolves |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 22 May :'''Venue:''' [[Halliwell Jones Stadium]] :'''Referee:''' B Thaler {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 4–6 :'''Attendance:''' 10,718 :'''[http://superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3089723 RFL report]''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | Warrington 16–8 WIGAN |- | {| border="0" width="100%" | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Tries:''' [[Chris Riley (rugby league)|C Riley]], [[Matt King (rugby player)|M King]], [[Michael Monaghan|M Monaghan]] | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Tries:''' [[Cameron Phelps|C Phelps]] |- | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Goals:''' [[Chris Bridge|C Bridge]] (2) | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Goals:''' [[Pat Richards|P Richards]] (2) |} |} |} Following his loan spell at Barrow, Lee Mossop transferred to Huddersfield Giants on a month-long loan.{{Cite web|url=http://www.liverpoolecho.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/2009/05/19/wigan-s-lee-mossop-joins-super-league-rivals-huddersfield-giants-100252-23658262/|title=Wigan's Lee Mossop joins Super League rivals Huddersfield Giants|publisher=[[Liverpool Echo]]|date=19 May 2009|access-date=22 September 2009}} Prior to Wigan's next match against Warrington Wolves, Noble, whose contract at Wigan was due to expire at the end of 2009, publicly admitted that he was becoming impatient with the lack of an offer from the club to extend his contract, after reports in the [[Sydney Morning Herald]] linked him to a coaching role at [[Sydney Roosters]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Noble-growing-impatient-over-Warriors.5294276.jp|title=Noble growing impatient over Warriors future|publisher=Wigan Today|date=22 May 2009|access-date=24 February 2010|first=Tony|last=Dewhurst}} Wigan had not won a game in Warrington since 2003, and despite leading for over half the match, failed to break this record.{{Cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_report/0,19936,11070_3089723,00.html|title=Wolves too fierce for Warrington|publisher=[[Sky Sports]]|date=23 May 2009|access-date=24 February 2010}} Warrington successfully charged down two kicks, before a kick from Thomas Leuluai on the third set of six tackles lead to Cameron Phelps scoring the match's opening try. Poor discipline from Ben Westwood gave a penalty, and Wigan almost took advantage when Joel Tomkins was held up over the try-line in the act of scoring by former Wigan full-back, Richie Mathers. Wigan again launched the next attack after forcing a goal line drop out, but a try from Amos Roberts was disallowed because of a knock-on by Pat Richards. Warrington then applied a sustained twenty minute period of pressure, but errors from Westwood and Briers kept them off the scoresheet. Briers weighted his next kick better, with Matt King out-jumping Shaun Ainscough to score Warrington's first try. Bridge's missed conversion meant that Wigan lead 6–4 at half-time, and a penalty kicked by Richards early in the second half meant that this lead was increased by two points. Warrington gained the lead after 55 minutes after Chris Riley punished Stuart Fielden and Andy Coley for giving away successive penalties with a try in the corner. Michael Monaghan shimmied past three Wigan defenders to finish the game off with another Warrington try. {{-}} {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#790363; color:#FFFFFF;"| Challenge Cup Quarter-final – Salford City Reds |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 29 May :'''Venue:''' [[JJB Stadium]] :'''Referee:''' T Alibert {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 10–6 :'''Attendance:''' 9,466 :'''[https://web.archive.org/web/20120308104828/http://www.therfl.co.uk/~therflc/results/report.php?z=challengecup&id=3146112 RFL report]''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | WIGAN 28–6 Salford |- | {| border="0" width="100%" | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Tries:''' [[Gareth Hock|G Hock]] (2), [[Pat Richards|P Richards]] (3) | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Tries:''' [[Luke Adamson|L Adamson]] |- | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Goals:''' P Richards 4 | width="50%" style="vertical-align:top;" | '''Goals:''' [[John Wilshere|J Wilshere]] |} |} |} Tim Smith had been left out of the tie against Warrington owing to a family illness, and did not return from his home country, Australia, in time for Wigan's Challenge Cup tie against Salford City Reds, but George Carmont returned from injury.{{Cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2009/may/25/castleford-wigan-challenge-cup|title=Challenge Cup injury gloom for Castleford and Wigan|work=[[The Guardian]]|date=25 May 2009|access-date=24 February 2010|first=Andy|last=Wilson | location=London}} Fielden was also forced to miss the game, after receiving a one-match ban for raising a knee in a tackle during the defeat to Warrington.{{Cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/fielden-appeals-ban-1691625.html|title=Fielden appeals ban|work=[[The Independent]]|date=28 May 2009|access-date=24 February 2010|first=Dave|last=Hadfield|location=London|archive-date=2 June 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090602083814/http://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/fielden-appeals-ban-1691625.html|url-status=dead}} Without Smith and Fielden, Wigan comprehensively beat Salford City Reds 28–6 to advance to the semi-finals stage of the tournament.{{Cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/8071490.stm|title=Wigan 28–6 Salford|publisher=[[British Broadcasting Corporation|BBC]]|date=29 May 2009|access-date=24 February 2010}} Gareth Hock gave the home side the lead, before Salford equalised with a try from Luke Adamson. Wigan replied by taking the lead with a George Carmont try, and could have scored again after Hock broke the line, but Sam Tomkins knocked-on. Another knock-on, this time from Richards, denied Wigan another try. Salford launched repeated attacks on the Wigan line early in the second half, through Mark Henry and Stefan Ratchford, before Willie Talau was pushed into touch by Sam Tomkins. A penalty from Richards added two more points to Wigan's total, and he soon scored his second try, followed by his third on 70 minutes for his second hat-trick in a Wigan jersey. Hock scored the final try, his second, after Tomkins had come close, to finish the game in favour of the Warriors. Wigan therefore entered their first Challenge Cup semi-final since 2007, when they were defeated by Catalans Dragons at the Halliwell Jones Stadium in Warrington,{{Cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2007/jul/30/rugbyleague.sport1|title=Catalans' triumph caps Wigan's week of woe|work=[[The Guardian]]|date=30 July 2007|access-date=24 February 2010|first=Andy|last=Wilson | location=London}} and it was Warrington Wolves who were drawn as their 2009 semi-final opponents.{{Cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/8076184.stm|title=Giants face Saints in semi-final|publisher=[[British Broadcasting Corporation|BBC]]|date=1 June 2009|access-date=24 February 2010}} ===June=== {{-}} {| class="toccolours collapsible collapsed" border="0" align="right" width="40%" style="margin:1em;" |- ! colspan=2 style="background:#D31718; color:#FFFFFF;"| Round 15 – Salford City Reds |- |{{Col-begin|width=100%}} {{Col-2}} :'''Date:''' 5 June :'''Venue:''' [[JJB Stadium]] :'''Referee:''' I Smith {{Col-2}} :'''Half-time:''' 28–6 :'''Attendance:''' 11,550 :'''[http://www.superleague.co.uk/report.php?id=3086347 RFL report]''' {{Col-end}} {| border=0 width=100% ! colspan=2 | '''WIGAN 34–18 Salford''' |- | '''Tries:''' [[George Carmont|G Carmont]]

It is Wigan's worst start to a season in 24 years. * Saturday 28 February – Sam Tomkins makes his début for Wigan against Harlequins RLFC. * Saturday 28 February – Wigan get their first win of the [[Super League XIV]] season by beating Harlequins RL twenty-four points to eighteen at the Twickenham Stoop * Sunday 5 April – Wigan defeat Barrow Raiders 32–20 to progress in the Challenge Cup. * Monday 6 April – Wigan sign Martin Gleeson from Warrington Wolves for a fee of £100,000+. * Monday 6 April – Wigan release Richie Mathers who later signs for Warrington Wolves. * Thursday 9 April – First time during the Super League era that a Wigan verses St Helens match is played on a Thursday night. * Thursday 9 April – Wigan lose 19–12 to St Helens at the JJB Stadium in the first Wigan verses St Helens derby of Super League XIV. * Wednesday 15 April – [[Darrell Goulding]] is released on loan to the [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]] and Lee Mossop joins the [[Barrow Raiders]] on loan. * Sunday 19 April – The first ever match between Wigan Warriors and Celtic Crusaders ends with Wigan winning 44–10. * Saturday 2 May – First 'Magic Weekend' at Murrayfield Stadium in Scotland where Wigan beat local rivals St Helens by 38 points to 18. * Saturday 9 May – Wigan defeat Wakefield Trinity Wildcats 28–17 to progress to the quarter-finals of the Challenge Cup. * Monday 18 May – Lee Mossop joins Super League team Huddersfield Giants on loan. * Friday 22 May – A 16–8 defeat against Warrington Wolves at the Halliwell Jones Stadium means Wigan have still never won at the Halliwell Jones Stadium in a competitive match. A record stretching back 6 years and including a 36–24 defeat to Catalans Dragons in a Challenge Cup Semi-final. * Friday 29 May – Wigan beat Salford City Reds 28–6 to progress to the semi-finals of the Challenge Cup. * Saturday 13 June – Mark Flanagan makes his Super League début for Wigan. * Saturday 13 June – Wigan's first game at Brewery Field ends in a 22–16 defeat. It is also the first time Wigan have lost to the Celtic Crusaders. * Tuesday 23 June – Gareth Hock tests positive for [[Benzoylecgonine]], a [[metabolite]] of [[cocaine]] and is provisionally [[Suspension (punishment)|suspended]] by the [[Rugby Football League]]. * Friday 24 July – In a match marketed as 'The BIG One' 20,295 people watch Wigan beat Leeds at the JJB Stadium which is the highest attendance between the two teams in Super League. * Friday 24 July – Wigan play their last match at the JJB Stadium before its official re-branding to the DW stadium on 1 August 2009. * Friday 31 July – Wigan's five game unbeaten run comes to an end with a 10–6 defeat to local rivals St Helens. * Saturday 8 August – Wigan are knocked out of the Challenge Cup by a 39–26 defeat to Warrington Wolves. }} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * ''[http://www.wiganwarriors.com/ Wigan Warriors official site]'' {{Wigan Warriors}} {{2009 in rugby league}} [[Category:Wigan Warriors seasons]] [[Category:2009 Super League season by club|Wigan Warriors season]]
